title: Somatic Therapy for Intergenerational Trauma: Healing What Was Passed Down
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy addresses intergenerational trauma by healing the body’s stored stress and nervous system dysregulation.
body:
Understanding Intergenerational Trauma: What Are We Really Carrying?

Intergenerational trauma refers to the transmission of trauma responses from one generation to the next. This isn’t just about stories passed down or family patterns—it’s about how trauma gets embedded in our nervous system and body, influencing behaviors and emotional responses long after the original events have passed. Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, a leading trauma researcher, emphasizes that trauma lives in the body, not just the mind. When trauma is unprocessed, it can manifest as chronic anxiety, hypervigilance, or emotional numbness that feels inexplicable but deeply familiar.

Even if your parents or grandparents never explicitly talked about their traumatic experiences, their bodies and nervous systems carry the imprints. These imprints can shape how they parented, how they related emotionally, and how they managed stress. This creates a biological and psychological inheritance that can limit your capacity to feel safe, regulate emotions, and form secure attachments.

The Science Behind Trauma Transmission

How does trauma get passed down? Research points to several pathways:

  • Epigenetics: Studies show that trauma can alter gene expression in ways that affect stress responses. For example, children of Holocaust survivors exhibited altered cortisol levels linked to trauma regulation (Yehuda et al., 2016).
  • Nervous System Dysregulation: Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how trauma disrupts the autonomic nervous system’s ability to regulate safety and social engagement. Parents’ chronic stress can create environments that inadvertently keep children’s nervous systems in a heightened state of alert.
  • Attachment Patterns: According to Daniel Siegel and Pat Ogden, trauma impacts the way caregivers attune to their children, often leading to insecure attachment styles that perpetuate emotional dysregulation.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Isn’t Enough

Many people who seek therapy for trauma find themselves stuck even after months or years of talk-based therapy. The reason is simple: trauma is stored in the body. As Francine Shapiro, the founder of EMDR, points out, trauma memories are often nonverbal and sensory-based. They live in somatic sensations, muscle tone, and autonomic responses rather than conscious narrative.

When therapy focuses primarily on cognitive reframing or verbal processing, it may bypass the core physiological component of trauma. This can leave the nervous system dysregulated and the body in a constant state of defense. As Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, teaches, healing trauma requires accessing the felt sense of the body to discharge trapped energy and reestablish nervous system balance.

Somatic Therapy: Healing Through the Body

Somatic therapy is a body-centered approach to trauma recovery that integrates neuroscience, psychology, and body awareness. It recognizes that trauma is held in muscle tension, breath patterns, posture, and nervous system states. By gently tuning into these bodily sensations, clients learn to track and regulate their internal experience.

Deb Dana, a leading clinician applying Polyvagal Theory in therapy, has shown that somatic work helps restore the nervous system’s natural flexibility. Clients learn to identify when they are in states of fight, flight, freeze, or social engagement and develop skills to shift towards safety and connection.

Somatic therapy techniques include mindful body scanning, breath regulation, movement, and guided awareness of sensations. This approach helps release immobilized trauma energy and rewires the nervous system to respond adaptively rather than reactively.

Breaking the Cycle: Somatic Therapy for Intergenerational Trauma

When it comes to intergenerational trauma, somatic therapy does more than soothe symptoms—it helps rewrite the biological legacy passed down through families. By working with the body’s implicit memory, somatic therapy can:

  • Disrupt automatic defense mechanisms inherited from previous generations.
  • Restore a sense of safety in the nervous system that may never have been fully established.
  • Enhance emotional regulation and resilience.
  • Improve relational patterns by fostering secure attachment within the present generation.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy combines somatic awareness with attachment theory, making it particularly effective for addressing intergenerational trauma. By helping clients track physical sensations linked to unresolved trauma, they gain agency over reactions that once felt automatic or unavoidable.

Integrating Somatic Practices Into Your Healing Journey

If you’re doing self-directed healing work, incorporating somatic practices can deepen your progress. Here are some actionable steps:

  • Practice Mindful Body Awareness: Spend 5-10 minutes daily noticing bodily sensations without judgment. This builds your capacity to recognize when trauma responses are activating.
  • Breath Regulation: Engage in slow, diaphragmatic breathing to activate the parasympathetic nervous system and promote calm.
  • Movement Exploration: Gentle movement like yoga, tai chi, or spontaneous shaking can help discharge stored tension.
  • Track Your Nervous System States: Learn to identify cues of fight, flight, freeze, or social engagement to respond instead of react.
  • Seek Somatic-Informed Therapists: When ready, work with professionals trained in somatic modalities like Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal-informed therapy.

Challenges and Considerations

Somatic therapy for intergenerational trauma is powerful but requires patience and safety. As Gabor Maté highlights, trauma healing is a process of slowly opening to painful material while maintaining connection to safety and support. It’s common to experience fluctuations in symptoms as stored trauma surfaces.

Establishing a strong therapeutic alliance and pacing the work according to your readiness helps prevent retraumatization. Always prioritize grounding and stabilization before diving into deeper trauma material.

“Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you.” – Gabor Maté

Looking Ahead: The Promise of Healing the Past Through the Body

Intergenerational trauma can feel like an invisible weight—an inheritance of suffering that shapes our experience without our permission. Somatic therapy offers a scientifically grounded path to unpack that weight, reclaim agency, and build resilience in the body and mind.

By addressing trauma where it lives—in the nervous system and body—we can finally interrupt the cycle and create new legacies of healing and connection for future generations.

For those who have tried talk therapy and still feel stuck, integrating somatic approaches offers a hopeful, embodied way forward.

References

  • Bessel van der Kolk, The Body Keeps the Score, 2014.
  • Peter Levine, Waking the Tiger, 1997.
  • Stephen Porges, The Polyvagal Theory, 2011.
  • Deb Dana, The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy, 2018.
  • Pat Ogden, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, 2006.
  • Gabor Maté, In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts, 2008.
  • Dan Siegel, The Developing Mind, 2012.
  • Francine Shapiro, EMDR Therapy, 2018.
  • Yehuda et al., "Epigenetic Biomarkers as Predictors and Correlates of PTSD Severity," Biological Psychiatry, 2016.

faqs.a: Intergenerational trauma is trauma that is passed down from one generation to the next, often through behavioral patterns, nervous system responses, and even epigenetic changes.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy help with intergenerational trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy helps by addressing trauma stored in the body and nervous system, allowing individuals to release chronic tension and regulate their emotional responses more effectively.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy be done without a therapist?
faqs.a: While self-directed somatic practices can be beneficial, working with a trained somatic therapist is recommended for safely navigating deeper trauma, especially intergenerational trauma.

faqs.q: How long does healing intergenerational trauma take?
faqs.a: Healing is highly individual and can take months or years. The process involves gradually building safety, awareness, and nervous system regulation.

metaDescription: Discover how somatic therapy heals intergenerational trauma by addressing trauma stored in the body and nervous system.
